feedback.text: Hello and a fine day to who might read my comment. I want to give my feedback concerning two choice matters that would be worthy to add to the Digital Fairness Act, those of the planned end of service for games and digital goods by their producing companies (in regard of the Stop Destroying Videogames movement) and the security/internet freedom crisis currently happening both in the UK and the US, crisis that I fear would have repercussions on the European “side” of the internet. Firstly, I completely endorse the Stop Killing Games/Stop Destroying Videogames movement, as I believe that a product, once bought, and despite not being physical, should be the property of its buyer and not tied to the whim of a producer. Yet, as you have been reminded recently by the SDV movement, the reality is completely different: Producers such as EA, Ubisoft, Nintendo and others (those three being the main culprits, by far...) developped an habit in creating games that require needless connection to their server to be accessed and played, only to cut this connection a few years after the release, once their profit is done, effectively destroying the game that should be the property of their buyer. In which example, you may have heard about the backlash stricking Nintendo, their Crusade against emulation, older games and the legaly and morally grey practice they tied to their new console the Switch II (i.e.: bricking the console of people via software updates). The second matter, and for me the most important one, is the new episode in the sempiternal war against internet liberty, freedom of speech and net Anonymity that is currently happening in the UK and US, spearheaded by companies like Google, Youtube, Mastercard, Visa who, under the convenient banner of making internet safer for children and cracking down NSFW material, are starting to require extremely personal, extremely sensitive datas to access content that has been, thus far, free and accessible. This measure is an atrocity as it tears down any effort made the past decades for personal security on the net and given power over our personal lives to private companies ill renowned for their illegal and morally grey use of personal datas as well as the numerous and frequent leaks they suffer regarding this information. I wish to say that the possibility of such an authoritarian move affecting european users via american websites seems to be all dystopian to me. I do not endorse the ‘Collectiveshout’ groupuscule that started this new Crusade, nor their astroturfing. I would appreciate if those two important matters were to be discussed during the commission, as I feel they are relevant (and critical) to the wellbeing of our freedom and security on the net, not only as users and consumers, but as human beings as well. I thank you for reading me, and wish you a pleasant day.
